title: A History of God: The 4,000-Year Quest of Judaism, Christianity and Islam
excerpt: A sweeping intellectual history tracing how the concept of God evolved across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam from Abraham to the modern age, arguing that ideas of the divine are pragmatic creations that change when they cease to work.
keyIdeas: Ideas of God are not static revelations but evolving human constructs shaped by historical circumstances
The three monotheistic faiths share a common root yet diverged dramatically in their conceptions of the divine
Mystical traditions across all three religions converge on an apophatic God beyond human Categories
The modern 'death of God' reflects the failure of a particular Western conception, not the end of religious yearning
keyTakeaways: God has been conceived as warrior, king, father, philosopher's abstraction, mystical nothingness, and personal companion at different historical moments
Greek philosophy fundamentally reshaped Jewish, Christian, and Islamic theology in parallel ways
Fundamentalism is a modern reaction, not a throwback to premodern religion
The most durable conceptions of God are those that address the existential needs of their time
whoShouldRead: Religious readers seeking historical perspective on their own tradition
Atheists and agnostics curious about how God-concepts developed
Students of comparative religion and intellectual history
Anyone puzzled by the persistence of religion in a secular age
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a theological defense of a particular doctrine
Those who prefer a narrow focus on a single tradition
Anyone expecting a proof for or against God's existence
